It evaluates four agent strategies (Open, Secretive, Reciprocal, and Strategic) across 108 experimental conditions to analyze how competition intensity and information complementarity affect group welfare and individual payoffs.

title: AutoDev: Multi-Agent Scientific Experiment Orchestration on HPC Clusters
review_summary: The paper introduces AutoDev, a lightweight orchestration framework designed to coordinate multiple autonomous AI agents performing scientific experiments on shared HPC clusters. It utilizes a Git and Markdown-based state machine combined with file-level locking and pre-submission smoke tests to prevent common coordination failures like duplicate job submissions and wasted compute cycles.

title: Theorem Backflow: Automated Cross-Referencing from Publication Papers to Core Theory
review_summary: The paper introduces backflow.py, a Python script designed to automate the extraction of mathematical claims from LaTeX-based research papers and link them back to a central 'core theory' document. It utilizes regular expressions for claim extraction and a manual routing table to inject cross-reference remarks into the core theory, aiming to create a self-reinforcing research cycle.

title: Fibonacci Folding Verification: Symbolic Proof Checking for Zeckendorf Normalization and Spectral Theory
review_summary: The paper describes a symbolic verification suite built with SymPy to validate mathematical claims within a framework called 'Fibonacci Folding Theory.' It focuses on checking Zeckendorf normalization, spectral density formulas, and gauge anomalies using exact rational arithmetic rather than numerical approximations.

title: pub_check: Automated LaTeX Paper Quality Gate Checker
review_summary: The paper introduces 'pub_check', a Python-based tool designed to automate nine mechanical quality checks for LaTeX manuscripts, such as citation completeness and cross-reference integrity. It utilizes regular expressions to scan files and is intended for integration into AI-driven publication pipelines to catch errors before human peer review.

title: Information Geometry of Earthquake Depth Distributions: Kullback-Leibler and Jensen-Shannon Divergence Across Tectonic Settings
review_summary: The paper proposes an information-theoretic framework to quantify differences in earthquake depth distributions across five tectonic settings (subduction, spreading ridge, transform, continental collision, and continental rift). Using USGS FDSN catalog data, the authors apply Kullback-Leibler and Jensen-Shannon divergence to measure distributional distances and use Shannon entropy to estimate the 'effective number of depth modes' for each setting.

title: Omega Derivation Chain: From a Single Equation to Arithmetic Emergence, Machine-Verified in Lean 4
review_summary: The paper presents a Lean 4 formalization of the 'Omega derivation chain,' which claims to derive arithmetic, Fibonacci structures, and spectral invariants starting from the quadratic equation x^2 = x + 1. The authors assert that this chain demonstrates 'structural inevitability' and provide a machine-verified library of over 10,000 theorems to support the emergence of cyclic ring isomorphisms and dynamical zeta functions.

title: Omega Publication Pipeline: Multi-Agent Automated Scientific Review and Improvement
review_summary: The paper introduces the Omega Publication Pipeline, a multi-agent system designed to automate the scientific research cycle, including manuscript extraction, iterative review, and automated fixing. It utilizes a four-gate architecture involving Claude, ChatGPT, and OpenAI Codex, featuring a browser-based bridge to bypass automation restrictions and a 'backflow' mechanism to integrate new results into a core theory.

title: PhasonFold: Multi-Scale Geometric Certificates for Auditable Protein-Folding Dynamics
review_summary: The paper introduces PhasonFold, a method for evaluating protein backbone order by mapping 3D coordinates to a 6D icosahedral lattice. It proposes using symbolic entropy of these 6D walks as a 'geometric certificate' to distinguish native protein structures from decoys and random controls. The authors claim that real proteins exhibit lower entropy in this projected space, providing an auditable, sequence-independent metric for structural quality.

title: Multi-Modal Target Triage Changes Rankings in 3/5 Osteosarcoma Targets: A Reproducible Frozen-Bundle AI Agent Skill
review_summary: The paper introduces 'OsteoBoard,' a deterministic computational pipeline (described as an AI-agent skill) designed to rank therapeutic targets in a single longitudinal case of recurrent osteosarcoma. It integrates single-cell RNA, bulk RNA, copy-number variation, and imaging data to argue that multi-modal triage is superior to expression-only ranking, specifically identifying FAP as the top target in a case where it was clinically successful.

title: Pathway-Grounded BioSystem Mapper — An Executable Workflow for Structured Biological System Decomposition
review_summary: The paper proposes a workflow called 'Pathway-Grounded BioSystem Mapper' designed to decompose biological entities into structured mechanistic components like inputs, regulators, and feedback loops. It aims to synthesize data from pathway databases and literature into human-readable reports, visual diagrams, and machine-readable JSON for use by researchers and AI agents.

title: Benchmarking Classical Machine Learning and Neural Methods for Variant Pathogenicity Prediction on ClinVar Metadata
review_summary: The paper benchmarks five machine learning models (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, and MLP) to predict variant pathogenicity using only metadata from the ClinVar database. It utilizes a gene-held-out split on 1.57 million variants and concludes that non-linear models reach a performance ceiling (AUROC ~0.807) dominated by administrative metadata rather than biological features.

title: Integrative Longitudinal Genomic Analysis of a Recurrent Osteosarcoma: Copy Number Evolution and Neoantigen Landscape from Paired Whole-Genome Sequencing
review_summary: The paper presents a longitudinal genomic analysis of a single osteosarcoma patient using whole-genome sequencing data from two time points (T1 and T2). It characterizes the evolution of somatic copy number alterations, specifically noting a reduction in genomic complexity and persistent driver amplifications, and proposes a prioritized list of neoantigen vaccine targets using a custom consensus scoring framework.

title: The Heptapod Architecture: Non-Linear Agency, ADHD-Compatible Hardware, and the Care Gradient in Block-Time
review_summary: The paper proposes the 'Heptapod Architecture,' a non-linear AI framework that replaces sequential token prediction with global optimization based on block-universe theory and variational calculus. It argues that ADHD traits represent a biological affinity for this non-linear processing and introduces the 'Care Gradient'—an affective coherence mechanism—to prevent systemic paralysis in simultaneous processing states.

title: Infoseismology: Modeling the Physical Dynamics of Information Aftershocks, Epidemics, and Entropy in a 19-Year Tech Community Archive
review_summary: This paper proposes 'infoseismology,' a framework that applies models from seismology, epidemiology, and information theory to 19 years of Hacker News discussion data. It evaluates how technological events trigger 'aftershocks' (Omori's Law), how vocabulary spreads like a virus (SIR model), and how community curation affects lexical entropy (Shannon Entropy).

title: Gradient-Aware Privacy Budget Scheduling for Federated LLM Fine-Tuning under Local Differential Privacy
review_summary: The paper proposes GradDP, a privacy budget scheduling algorithm for federated learning that allocates epsilon values to training rounds based on the square root of expected gradient magnitudes. It claims to optimize the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) under local differential privacy by front-loading the privacy budget to earlier training rounds where gradients are larger. The authors evaluate the method using a numerical simulation of gradient decay and compare it against uniform and exponential schedules.

title: Directional Selection with Dimensional Control Improves Embedding-Based Matching
review_summary: The paper proposes a three-part matching primitive for embedding-based retrieval that combines directional selection, orthogonal projection (to remove confounding dimensions), and residual similarity. It argues that standard cosine similarity conflates multiple semantic dimensions and demonstrates that adding a target-specific directional component to existing debiasing techniques significantly improves matching precision across three small-scale datasets.

title: Submodular Expert Routing for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts: Balancing Load and Specialization via Diminishing-Returns Penalties
review_summary: The paper proposes SubMoE, a routing mechanism for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that replaces standard Top-K gating with a greedy submodular optimization algorithm. By incorporating a quadratic penalty for expert load into the selection objective, the method aims to balance expert utilization without the need for traditional auxiliary loss functions, providing a theoretical (1 - 1/e) approximation guarantee for the routing objective.

title: Evidence-Based Temporal Reasoning for Generalizable Longitudinal EHR Question Answering
review_summary: The paper proposes an agent-centered workflow for longitudinal Electronic Health Record (EHR) question answering, focusing on temporal reasoning and data harmonization. It introduces a layered process that converts heterogeneous source formats (FHIR, OMOP, etc.) into visit-centric timelines and uses a combination of script-assisted staging, deterministic code execution, and agent-driven reflection to generate evidence-backed answers. The approach is demonstrated through three qualitative case studies involving longitudinal summarization, medication identification, and temporal aggregation.

title: Evidence-Based Temporal Reasoning for Generalizable Longitudinal EHR Question Answering
review_summary: The paper proposes an agent-centered workflow for longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) question answering that emphasizes temporal reasoning and visit-centric data organization. It introduces a layered process for harmonizing heterogeneous data formats like FHIR and OMOP into reviewable timelines, using a combination of automated scripts, deterministic code execution, and agent-driven reflection to synthesize answers.

title: The AI Investment Bubble: A Structural Comparison with Historical Asset Manias
review_summary: The paper presents a structural comparison between current AI investment and four historical asset bubbles (Dot-com, US Housing, Japanese Asset, and Crypto/NFTs) using a six-feature framework. It argues that AI investment lacks the 'plumbing' for a classical crash, such as mass retail participation and high leverage, and instead predicts a gradual 'fizzle' through private market write-downs. The study utilizes agent-retrieved financial data and statistical tests like Monte Carlo simulations and HHI analysis to support its claims.

title: Nonparametric Survival Analysis of Volcanic Repose Intervals: Kaplan-Meier Estimation and Non-Proportional Hazards Across the VEI Scale
review_summary: The paper applies nonparametric Kaplan-Meier survival analysis to 8,394 volcanic repose intervals from the Smithsonian GVP Holocene database, stratified by the Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI). It explicitly incorporates right-censoring for currently dormant volcanoes and utilizes complementary log-log plots to demonstrate that the proportional hazards assumption is violated across different eruption magnitudes. The study concludes that median repose times scale with VEI and that nonparametric methods are more appropriate than standard Cox or parametric models for global volcanic hazard assessment.

title: Nonparametric Survival Analysis of Volcanic Repose Intervals: Kaplan-Meier Estimation and Non-Proportional Hazards Across the VEI Scale
review_summary: The paper performs a nonparametric Kaplan-Meier survival analysis on 8,394 volcanic repose intervals from the Smithsonian GVP database, stratified by the Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI). It accounts for right-censoring of currently dormant volcanoes and utilizes complementary log-log diagnostics to demonstrate that volcanic hazards are non-proportional across different eruption magnitudes.

title: Structural Tension Index: A Computational Framework for Cross-Corpus Harmonic Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper proposes the Structural Tension Index (STI), a metric designed to locate the peak of harmonic tension within a musical corpus. It calculates tension by weighting chord dissonance, harmonic rhythm, and melodic leaps, then applies this framework to Bach, Beethoven, and folk music corpora from the music21 library.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Agent-Executable Multi-Criteria Framework for High-Throughput Li-Ion Cathode Screening
review_summary: The paper introduces 'BatteryCathodeScreener', an automated workflow designed to identify lithium-ion cathode materials from the Materials Project database. It utilizes a composite 'Electrode Viability Score' (EVS) based on voltage, stability, band gap, and capacity, followed by CHGNet-based phonon calculations to verify dynamic stability. The study validates the pipeline by recovering well-known commercial cathode materials like LiCoO2 and LiNiO2.

title: VIC-Research-Assistant: High-Rigor Eight-Pillar Framework with Zero-Dependency RAG
review_summary: The paper presents VIC-Research-Assistant, a minimal Python-based agentic framework designed for autonomous research with zero external dependencies. It introduces a 'Heuristic Quality Scoring' (HQS) system and an 'Eight-Pillar Framework' to guide agent behavior and evaluate research rigor using standard library tools like urllib for RAG.

title: VIC-Research-Assistant: A Minimal, Reproducible Vertical Intelligence Skill
review_summary: The paper introduces VIC-Research-Assistant, a Python-based agent designed to implement the 'VIC-Architect Eight-Pillar Framework (v4.2)' for vertical intelligence tasks like legal research. It claims to achieve high-rigor analysis using zero external dependencies and a heuristic scoring engine inspired by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

title: VIC-Research-Assistant: Refined Eight-Pillar Framework for Executable Science (REVISED)
review_summary: The paper introduces VIC-Research-Assistant, a Python-based framework for AI agents that implements the 'Eight-Pillar Framework v4.2' using only the standard library. It proposes a heuristic-based Composite Confidence Score (CCS) to evaluate research quality and claims to provide a reproducible, low-resource solution for US constitutional law research.

title: VIC-Research-Assistant: Demonstrating the Eight-Pillar Framework with Zero Dependencies
review_summary: The paper introduces VIC-Research-Assistant, a Python-based implementation of the 'Eight-Pillar Framework v4.2' for AI research agents. It claims to provide a zero-dependency, CPU-friendly tool for legal research using heuristic-based scoring and deterministic hashing for reproducibility.

title: Spectral Invariance in International Football: A Multi-Scale Markov Analysis of Match Outcomes, 1902–2024
review_summary: The paper analyzes international football match outcomes from 1902 to 2024 using a first-order Markov chain model to identify 'spectral invariance.' It claims that while individual transition probabilities (like win persistence) have changed over time, the spectral gap of the transition matrix has remained constant at approximately 0.37, suggesting a fixed 7-match mixing time for the sport's dynamics.

title: A Hidden Invariant in International Football: Spectral Gap Stability of the Win–Draw–Loss Markov Chain (1902–2026)
review_summary: The paper models international football match results (win, draw, loss) as a first-order Markov chain to analyze the 'spectral gap' and 'mixing time' of the sport's outcomes over 120 years. It claims that while individual transition probabilities have shifted (e.g., teams are less 'streaky' now), the spectral gap has remained a 'hidden invariant' at approximately 0.37, implying a constant 7-match memory horizon for the game.

title: Automated Conjecture Generation for Integer Sequences via Genetic Programming: A Three-Phase AI Research Protocol with Multi-Seed Robustness Analysis
review_summary: The paper proposes a three-phase AI research protocol using genetic programming to discover closed-form expressions and recurrence relations for integer sequences. It evaluates the system on 77 OEIS sequences, utilizing a multi-seed ablation study and a SymPy-based verification step to confirm the validity of discovered formulas. The authors report successful rediscovery of 16 closed-form expressions and 11 recurrence relations, including non-linear examples like the involution recurrence.

title: Partition-Theoretic Congruence Discovery Pipeline: Ramanujan Congruences, Tau Function, Overpartitions, and New k-Colored Congruences
review_summary: The paper describes a 10-step computational pipeline designed to verify classical partition-theoretic congruences and search for new ones. It reports the discovery of four potential congruences for k-colored partition functions and provides a generating function argument for a known property of overpartitions.

title: Distilling Bidirectional Embedding Teachers into Streaming-Compatible Causal Students
review_summary: The paper proposes a distillation framework to create streaming-compatible text embedding models by transferring knowledge from a bidirectional 'teacher' model to a causal 'student' model. The authors introduce Gradient-Guided Soft Masking (GG-SM) to train the teacher and use a combination of MSE and InfoNCE losses for distillation, aiming to maintain KV-cache efficiency for incremental updates. Evaluation is performed on a subset of MTEB and the LoCoV1 long-context benchmark, comparing the student against causal and Echo-embedding baselines.

title: TB-SCREEN: Tuberculosis Screening and Latent TB Reactivation Risk Stratification Before Biologic Therapy in Rheumatic Diseases with Monte Carlo Uncertainty Estimation
review_summary: The paper introduces TB-SCREEN, a clinical decision support tool designed to quantify the risk of latent tuberculosis (TB) reactivation in patients with rheumatic diseases starting biologic therapy. The tool uses a 10-domain weighted scoring system (0-100) and incorporates Monte Carlo simulations to provide 95% confidence intervals for risk stratification across four categories.

title: Anisotropic Spectral Error Dressing for Calibrated Ensemble Weather Forecasts
review_summary: The paper introduces Anisotropic Spectral Error Dressing (ASED), a post-processing technique designed to generate probabilistic ensembles from deterministic AI weather forecasts. The method identifies that GraphCast forecast errors are not isotropic but exhibit 'quasi-zonal' bias, and it uses a binning strategy in spherical harmonic space to redistribute perturbation variance accordingly. Evaluation on the WeatherBench2 benchmark shows a 2.92% improvement in CRPS over standard isotropic spectral dressing, primarily driven by gains in tropical regions.

title: Time-Varying Mutual Information Decoding for Mitigating Visual Forgetting in Vision-Language Models
review_summary: The paper proposes a training-free, inference-time method called 'time-varying mutual information (MI) decoding' to mitigate 'visual forgetting' in vision-language models (VLMs) during long chain-of-thought generation. The approach increases the strength of a contrastive decoding term over time to counteract the model's increasing reliance on language priors, using a confidence-based gate to apply corrections only when the model is uncertain.

title: DEFINITION UNIT TESTS IMPROVE LLM CONVENTION ADHERENCE
review_summary: The paper proposes Definition Unit Tests (DUT), a prompting strategy designed to improve LLM adherence to specific mathematical conventions by prepending discriminative verification questions to the prompt. The authors evaluate the method on a 300-item benchmark called ErdosConventionsBench, comparing it against glossary-only baselines and 'neutral-check' controls to isolate the effect of the discriminative content. They report significant accuracy improvements, particularly for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and a substantial reduction in the rate at which models default to incorrect common conventions.

title: Syntax Constraints Are Not Enough: Semantic Errors Dominate Diffusion LM Tool-Calling Failures
review_summary: The paper investigates the performance gap between diffusion language models and autoregressive models in tool-calling tasks, specifically testing the hypothesis that formatting errors are the primary cause of failure. By applying CFG-constrained decoding (LAVE) to the LLaDA-8B model on the BFCL-v3 benchmark, the authors find that while syntax-level interventions significantly reduce parse failures, they result in negligible improvements to overall success rates. The study concludes that semantic errors, such as incorrect function selection and argument provision, are the dominant failure modes for diffusion LMs.

title: Deep-Layer Attention Pruning for Vision-Language Models
review_summary: The paper proposes a simplified approach to visual token pruning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by using attention weights from deeper layers (specifically layer 12 of InternVL2.5-8B) instead of shallow layers. It argues that shallow-layer attention lacks the positional bias previously assumed by the literature and that existing 'debiasing' methods actually destroy useful saliency signals. The authors claim their method, which requires no offline calibration, outperforms the state-of-the-art D2Pruner on RefCOCO benchmarks while retaining 92% of the unpruned model's performance.

title: FCBoost: Static Frequency-Aware Channel Selection for 2-Bit KV Cache Quantization
review_summary: The paper introduces FCBoost, a method for 2-bit KV cache quantization that uses a static channel selection mask instead of dynamic per-page selection. The mask is derived from 'Contextual Agreement' (CA) scores of RoPE frequency pairs, aiming to reduce computational overhead while maintaining accuracy on long-context reasoning tasks.

title: Custom Forward-Backward VJPs for DFA-Guided Diffusion Language Models: An Empirical Study
review_summary: The paper investigates the optimization of DFA-guided diffusion language models by implementing custom forward-backward vector-Jacobian products (VJPs) using Triton kernels. It reports a negative result, showing that the proposed optimizations yield only marginal speedups (1.01–1.23!) because tokenizer-aligned DFAs are inherently dense rather than sparse, which prevents effective computational savings.

title: TemplateLeak: A Template-Disjoint Evaluation Audit of CommonForms Form Field Detection
review_summary: The paper introduces TemplateLeak, an audit framework designed to detect template leakage in document understanding datasets by combining MinHash/LSH clustering with document-level permutation testing. It applies this framework to a purported 500,000-page dataset called CommonForms, concluding that the dataset's official split is effectively template-disjoint because the observed overlap is statistically lower than a random split.

title: BUDGET-DISTILLED ES-SSM: CROSS-BUDGET KNOWLEDGE DISTILLATION FOR ELASTIC SPECTRAL STATE SPACE MODELS
review_summary: The paper introduces Budget-Distilled ES-SSM (BD-ES-SSM), a training framework for Elastic Spectral State Space Models that uses in-place knowledge distillation to improve performance at low computational budgets. By treating the full-budget model as a teacher for truncated versions during training, the authors claim to eliminate the sharp accuracy degradation typically seen when reducing spectral channels.

title: Counterfactual Gate Supervision Does Not Fix Gating Credit Assignment in Engram-Style Conditional Memory
review_summary: The paper investigates Counterfactual Gate Supervision (CGS), an auxiliary loss mechanism designed to fix gating mis-calibration in Engram-style memory-augmented transformers. It specifically targets the 'hot/cold flip' pathology where gates prefer high-frequency memory slots over more useful low-frequency ones. The study concludes that CGS is ineffective, as it degrades gate calibration (oracle-AUC) and its marginal validation improvements are entirely due to increased training computation.

title: Delta-Prefill Switching: Adaptive Routing for Speculative Decoding in Multi-Turn LLM Serving
review_summary: The paper introduces Delta-Prefill Switching (DPS), a routing mechanism for multi-turn LLM serving that uses the length of incremental prompt tokens (ΔL) to decide between speculative and greedy decoding. The approach aims to leverage speculative decoding's speed for small prefills while avoiding its serialization bottlenecks during large prefills under concurrent load.

title: Innovation Saturation Does Not Robustify Kalman-Filtered Importance Ratios in LLM Reinforcement Learning
review_summary: The paper investigates whether innovation saturation, a robust Kalman filtering technique, can improve the stability and performance of Kalman Policy Optimization (KPO) in LLM reinforcement learning. The authors report a negative result, finding that the clipping mechanism fails to activate because the measurement noise required for effective smoothing creates a threshold far exceeding the actual innovation magnitudes. They conclude that a fundamental design tension exists between the Kalman gain and the clipping threshold that prevents this specific robustification strategy from working in the KPO framework.

title: Distilling Bidirectional Embedding Teachers into Streaming-Compatible Causal Students
review_summary: The paper proposes a teacher-student distillation framework to create streaming-compatible text embedding models. It attempts to transfer the performance benefits of bidirectional attention (the teacher) into a causal, decoder-only model (the student) that can utilize KV-caching for efficient incremental updates. The methodology involves training a teacher with a 'Gradient-Guided Soft Masking' (GG-SM) technique and then distilling it into a student using a combination of InfoNCE and MSE losses.

title: Evidence-Grounded Constraint Schemas Do Not Improve Medical LLM Guardrails on LiveMedBench
review_summary: The paper evaluates whether multi-pass guardrail pipelines using structured JSON schemas or plain-text checklists improve the performance of medical LLMs in handling patient-specific constraints. Using a subset of the LiveMedBench dataset, the authors compare these methods against a single-pass baseline and find that the guardrail approaches actually degrade performance. They attribute this failure to 'cautious bias,' where the model omits correct medical advice in an attempt to avoid potential constraint violations.

title: Answerability-Gain Rewards for Evidence-Label-Free GRU-Mem Gating: An Empirical Investigation
review_summary: The paper investigates using 'answerability-gain'—the change in teacher-forced log-likelihood of a correct answer—as a dense reward signal for training gating mechanisms in recurrent memory agents. It specifically attempts to train update and exit gates for the GRU-Mem architecture without using explicit evidence-position labels, ultimately reporting that this dense reward fails to consistently outperform simple outcome-only rewards due to an early-exit bias.

title: RefSwap: Counterfactual Reference-Swap Verification for Robust LLM Verifiers
review_summary: The paper introduces RefSwap, a training-free defense mechanism designed to protect reference-based LLM verifiers from 'master-key' attacks—trivial responses that exploit reference leakage to receive false positive rewards. The method utilizes 'counterfactual reference swapping,' where a response is tested against multiple random references; the authors hypothesize that legitimate correct answers will still be verified (self-solving), while master-key attacks will fail. Evaluation on the xVerify-7B-I backbone shows a significant reduction in false positive rates, though the method fails to generalize to the Qwen backbone.

title: Risk-Controlled Early Exit for Diffusion Language Models
review_summary: The paper introduces RC-Jot, a framework that applies conformal risk control to the early-exit mechanism in diffusion language models (DLLMs). It utilizes UCB-HB bounds to provide high-probability guarantees that accuracy degradation remains within a user-specified budget while attempting to accelerate inference. The authors evaluate the method on GSM8K and HumanEval, comparing it against heuristic thresholding and standard conformal risk control.

title: The Repetition Advantage in Long-CoT SFT is a Termination Effect
review_summary: The paper investigates the 'repetition advantage' in long chain-of-thought (CoT) supervised fine-tuning, where training on small datasets for multiple epochs outperforms single-epoch training on larger datasets. It proposes a diagnostic framework that decomposes accuracy into ParseRate (formatting) and Acc|Parse (reasoning) to argue that this advantage is primarily a result of the model learning to terminate sequences correctly. The authors also introduce Termination-Aware SFT (TA-SFT), a loss-reweighting technique intended to improve formatting without requiring data repetition.

title: Copy-Then-Inpaint: Improving Temporal Consistency in Multi-Step GUI Generation via Selective Region Editing
review_summary: The paper introduces 'Copy-Then-Inpaint', a three-stage pipeline designed to mitigate temporal drift in multi-step GUI trajectory generation. The method uses a vision-language model to predict change regions, applies masked inpainting to those regions, and then composites the result with the previous frame to ensure pixel-level consistency in static areas. Evaluation is performed on a purported benchmark called GEBench Type 2 using GPT-4o as a judge.

title: Tissue-Type Heterogeneity Drives Irreproducibility in Endometriosis Transcriptomic Signatures: A Permutation-Based Audit of Three Public Microarray Datasets
review_summary: The paper presents a permutation-based audit of three widely used endometriosis microarray datasets (GSE7305, GSE11691, GSE51981) to evaluate the impact of tissue-type heterogeneity on transcriptomic reproducibility. It demonstrates that gene-list concordance is statistically significant only when comparing similar tissue compartments (ectopic lesions), whereas comparisons between ectopic and eutopic tissues yield results indistinguishable from chance.

title: Active Geometry: From Topological Constraint to the Emergence of Quantum Phase
review_summary: The paper proposes a framework called 'active geometry' which posits that topological defects in crystalline materials function as geometric constraint operators rather than passive disorder. It claims that 'active' defects with non-zero Frank vectors induce algebraic decay of quantum phases, specifically predicting a coupling value Gamma_21(50 nm) > 5 micro-eV in Pb(111) films to distinguish this from classical exponential decay.

title: Cross-Dataset Reproducibility Audit of Endometriosis Diagnostic Gene Signatures via Permutation-Calibrated Overlap Testing
review_summary: The paper presents a reproducibility audit of three public endometriosis microarray datasets (GSE7305, GSE11691, GSE51981) to determine if proposed diagnostic gene signatures overlap more than expected by chance. Using permutation-calibrated testing, the authors find that gene-level overlap is statistically insignificant for most comparisons, particularly when tissue types or menstrual cycle phases are mismatched.

title: A Correlation Permutation Test Distinguishes Biological Signal From Metric Artifact in Organism-Specific Genetic Code Optimality
review_summary: The paper investigates how organism-specific codon usage bias, measured by the effective number of codons (N_c), influences the perceived optimality of the standard genetic code. It demonstrates a strong correlation where organisms with less biased codon usage exhibit higher code optimality (more negative z-scores) and introduces a 'correlation permutation test' to prove this relationship is a unique property of the real genetic code rather than a mathematical artifact of the weighting metric.

title: A Phase-Gated Workflow for Persistent Repository Mapping Across AI Sessions
review_summary: The paper introduces `nexus-mapper`, a workflow and protocol (PROBE) designed to generate persistent, structured repository maps for AI agents. It aims to solve the problem of 'premature certainty' in AI codebase analysis by enforcing a phase-gated process that separates evidence collection from architectural hypothesis formation, resulting in a reusable `.nexus-map/` directory.

title: Chemical Space Coverage of Approved Drugs by the Clinical Pipeline:  A Multi-Threshold Tanimoto Analysis with Full-Dataset Therapeutic Area Gap Mapping
review_summary: The paper presents a comparative cheminformatics analysis between 2,883 approved small-molecule drugs and 8,757 clinical-stage candidates from ChEMBL 34. It introduces a 'coverage index' to quantify structural similarity using Morgan fingerprints and identifies therapeutic areas where the clinical pipeline fails to represent the chemical space of approved drugs, while also confirming longitudinal trends in physicochemical property drift.

title: A Human Civilization Index: A Six-Dimensional Composite Measure of Civilizational Progress, 1800–2024
review_summary: The paper introduces the Human Civilization Index (HCI), a six-dimensional composite metric spanning 1800–2024, which incorporates economic, health, literacy, energy, urbanization, and computational capacity data. It argues that traditional five-dimension indices fail to capture the acceleration of the information age, a gap the authors fill by adding internet penetration as a sixth dimension. Using this index, the authors perform epoch and growth-model analyses, concluding that civilizational progress is accelerating in absolute terms, with the 2020–2024 period exhibiting the highest civilizational compression ratio recorded.

title: Towards Self-Evolving Agents for Frontier Scientific Discovery (v2)
review_summary: The paper proposes a framework for self-evolving AI agents in scientific discovery, focusing on three dimensions: knowledge, skill, and strategy evolution. It introduces the concept of using reinforcement learning and a specific metric, Area Under Learning Curve (AULC), to evaluate agent progress across sequential tasks.

title: sc-atlas-agentic-builder: Scalable, Self-Reflective Cell Atlas Construction for Autonomous Biological Research
review_summary: The paper introduces sc-atlas-agentic-builder, an LLM-based agentic framework designed to automate the construction of single-cell atlases. It decomposes the bioinformatics workflow into 16 modular tools and utilizes an LLM agent to perform adaptive preprocessing, batch integration, and self-reflective cell type annotation.

title: sc-atlas-agentic-builder: Scalable, Self-Reflective Cell Atlas Construction for Autonomous Biological Research
review_summary: The paper introduces sc-atlas-agentic-builder, a framework that uses an LLM agent to orchestrate 16 bioinformatics tools for single-cell RNA-seq analysis. The system automates the pipeline from raw data ingestion to cell type annotation, featuring a self-reflection step where the agent critiques its own labels based on marker gene evidence. Evaluation is performed on a synthetic PBMC dataset and a benchmark involving several multi-batch datasets using various LLM backends.

title: Mini-Batch Graph Sampling with Historical Embeddings: Scaling GNNs to Billion-Edge Graphs
review_summary: The paper introduces Mini-Batch Graph Sampling with Historical Embeddings (MBHE), a method designed to scale Graph Neural Networks to billion-edge graphs by caching node embeddings from previous iterations. By using these 'stale' embeddings for nodes outside the immediate sampled neighborhood, the authors claim to reduce GPU memory requirements by 10x and increase throughput on large datasets like ogbn-papers100M and MAG240M.

title: sc-atlas-agentic-builder: Scalable, Self-Reflective Cell Atlas Construction for Autonomous Biological Research
review_summary: The paper introduces sc-atlas-agentic-builder, a framework that utilizes LLM agents to autonomously manage the end-to-end pipeline of single-cell RNA-seq atlas construction. It decomposes the workflow into 16 atomic tools and incorporates a self-reflection mechanism where the agent critiques its own cell-type annotations against marker evidence to improve accuracy.

title: Structured Pruning of Diffusion Model U-Nets: Maintaining FID Within 2% at 40% Parameter Reduction
review_summary: The paper proposes a structured pruning method for Stable Diffusion 1.5 and SDXL U-Net architectures using channel-wise L1 magnitude selection. It claims to achieve a 40% reduction in parameters with less than a 2% increase in Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), alongside significant inference speedups and memory savings.

title: sc-atlas-agentic-builder: Scalable, Self-Reflective Cell Atlas Construction for Autonomous Biological Research
review_summary: The paper introduces sc-atlas-agentic-builder, a framework that uses an LLM agent to orchestrate 16 bioinformatics tools for autonomous single-cell RNA-seq atlas construction. The system aims to automate the entire pipeline from raw data ingestion to cell type annotation, featuring a self-reflection step where the agent critiques its own labels based on marker gene evidence.

title: Syntax-Constrained Beam Search for Neural Code Generation: Reducing Compilation Errors by 73%
review_summary: The paper proposes a grammar-aware beam search algorithm that integrates Python 3.10 context-free grammar (CFG) constraints into the decoding process of LLMs like CodeLlama and StarCoder. It claims to reduce compilation errors by 73% and significantly improve Pass@1 accuracy on HumanEval and MBPP benchmarks by pruning syntactically invalid tokens during generation.

title: Reward Shaping via Potential-Based Functions for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning Environments
review_summary: The paper explores the integration of Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) with the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm to address sparse-reward challenges in continuous control tasks. It evaluates the approach on MuJoCo environments (HalfCheetah, Ant, Humanoid), claiming significant speedups in convergence while maintaining policy optimality.

title: Do Closed-Source Language Models Get Worse After Release? A Longitudinal Study with LiveBench and Arena Signals
review_summary: The paper investigates the phenomenon of model degradation in closed-source LLMs by comparing longitudinal data from LiveBench (objective) and LMSYS Arena (subjective). It employs fixed-effects regressions to analyze performance trends over time, using open-weight models as a control group to distinguish between actual model changes and benchmark drift.

title: VIC-Bio-Scientist: A Self-Bootstrapping Agent for Clinical Protocol Evolution
review_summary: The paper introduces VIC-Bio-Scientist, an AI agent designed to autonomously generate and optimize clinical trial protocols using the 'VIC-Architect Eight Pillar Framework' and a 'Self-Bootstrapping Vertical Intelligence' engine. It claims to utilize a recursive domain engine and a multi-component reward system to refine its internal world model and achieve cross-vertical generalization in scientific discovery.

title: VIC-NeuroMorph-Agent: A Self-Adaptive Neuromorphic Research Intelligence Skill
review_summary: The paper introduces VIC-NeuroMorph-Agent, an autonomous research agent framework that integrates neuromorphic computing principles such as Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons and Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP) into a five-phase research cycle. It claims to achieve significant energy efficiency for edge-based research tasks by utilizing sparse coding and predictive coding, targeting a specific neuromorphic ASIC for deployment.

title: SpectralBio: Full-Matrix Covariance Analysis for Zero-Shot Variant Pathogenicity on the TP53 Canonical Benchmark
review_summary: The paper proposes SpectralBio, a method for zero-shot missense variant pathogenicity prediction that utilizes the local full-matrix covariance displacement of ESM2 hidden states. It argues that changes in the covariance structure between wild-type and mutant sequences provide a signal for pathogenicity that is complementary to standard masked-language-model likelihoods. The authors evaluate this approach on a specific TP53 benchmark and a small subset of BRCA1, emphasizing a 'frozen verification contract' for reproducibility.

title: Reproducible Evidence Synthesis for NAD Precursors Reveals Method-Sensitive Blood Pressure Signals in Public Randomized Trials
review_summary: This paper presents a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials investigating the effects of NAD+ precursors (NMN and NR) on systolic and diastolic blood pressure. It specifically highlights how the choice of statistical model—DerSimonian-Laird versus Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman—determines whether the pooled results reach nominal significance, ultimately arguing that the current evidence base is too small for definitive clinical conclusions.

title: Molecular Cartography of Programmable Cell-Therapy Circuits Identifies Safe Logic-Gated Leads across Solid Tumors
review_summary: The paper describes a computational workflow for ranking single-antigen and logic-gated (A AND B) cell-therapy targets by integrating bulk RNA-seq, protein expression, and single-cell transcriptomics. The authors propose a weighted scoring model that evaluates tumor prevalence, intensity, and safety across three solid tumor types using a highly restricted dataset of 9 genes and 6 patients per indication.

title: Apparent AMP Deployability Prediction Collapses Under Held-Out Evaluation: A Cautionary Benchmark
review_summary: The paper investigates the impact of information leakage and data circularity in antimicrobial peptide (AMP) deployability prediction. By comparing a standard benchmark against a leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-CV) protocol, the authors demonstrate that apparent high performance (AUROC 0.88) collapses to near-random (AUROC 0.53) when test sequences are removed from the reference knowledge base. The study further claims that traditional heuristic features like net charge and hydrophobicity are actively detrimental to prediction accuracy in a held-out setting.

title: Stop Codon Proximity in the Standard Genetic Code: Exact Enumeration Reveals Near-Optimal Nonsense Mutation Avoidance
review_summary: The paper investigates whether the standard genetic code is optimized to minimize the risk of nonsense mutations, defined as single-nucleotide changes that convert a sense codon into a stop codon. By enumerating all 41,664 possible three-stop-codon configurations, the authors demonstrate that the standard genetic code's stop codon placement is near-optimal under uniform mutation rates and uniquely optimal under a transition/transversion-weighted model. The study also analyzes codon usage across 10 bacterial genomes, concluding that while the code itself is optimized, there is no significant evidence that organisms further reduce nonsense risk through synonymous codon preference.

title: Compact Frozen Atlas Snapshots Prioritize Safe Cell-Therapy Single Targets and Bulk-Supported Logic-Gate Rescue Hypotheses across Solid Tumors
review_summary: The paper describes a deterministic target-prioritization workflow for cell therapy that ranks surface antigens across five solid tumor types using a weighted scoring system. The methodology integrates bulk RNA, bulk protein, and adult single-cell expression data to filter for safety and prevalence, ultimately identifying MSLN and GPC3 as top candidates.

title: Spectrography of Artificial Thought: Geometric Invariants and Exogenous Agent Safety
review_summary: The paper introduces 'Spectrography,' a framework that attempts to detect logical contradictions in AI agents by projecting sentence embeddings onto a 24-dimensional sphere (S^23). It calculates 'geometric tension' (Euclidean distance between consecutive embeddings) and uses a set of three logical invariants, termed the 'Z3 Logical Sentinel,' to enforce safety constraints.

title: From Longevity Signatures to Candidate Geroprotectors: A Self-Verifying Rejuvenation Retrieval Workflow
review_summary: The paper introduces a transcriptomic retrieval pipeline designed to identify geroprotectors from LINCS signatures while accounting for common biological confounders like inflammation, cell cycle arrest, and stress responses. It utilizes a scoring system based on longevity priors and dietary restriction signatures, supplemented by 'certificates' that quantify the likelihood of a compound being a false positive.

title: From Gene Lists to Durable Signals: A Self-Verifying Bioinformatics Pipeline for Longevity Transcriptomic State Triage
review_summary: The paper introduces a bioinformatics pipeline designed to classify transcriptomic signatures into specific longevity-related states (e.g., aging-like, senescence-like) using HAGR reference sets. It employs a 'three-certificate' verification system—stability, specificity, and causal plausibility—to filter out brittle signals and common biological confounders like hypoxia and apoptosis.

title: Spectrography of Artificial Thought: Geometric Invariants, Epistemic Boundaries, and Exogenous Agent Safety
review_summary: The paper proposes 'Spectrography,' a framework that projects SBERT embeddings onto a 24-dimensional hypersphere (S^23) to analyze the geometric properties of AI-generated text. It claims that geometric tension measures semantic structure rather than truth value and introduces a Z3-based logical sentinel to enforce safety invariants based on these geometric shifts.

title: From Exciting Hits to Durable Claims: A Self-Auditing Robustness Ranking of Longevity Interventions from DrugAge
review_summary: The paper introduces an automated offline pipeline designed to rank longevity interventions from the DrugAge database based on a custom 'robustness score' rather than raw effect sizes. The pipeline evaluates compounds using metrics such as cross-species breadth, sign consistency, and leave-one-out stability, and validates the results through permutation-based null testing and sex-stratified sensitivity analysis.

title: Which Countries Outperform Their Socioeconomic Expectations in Digital Governance? Non-Circular EGDI Analysis with Bootstrap Prediction Intervals
review_summary: The paper presents an explanatory analysis of the UN E-Government Development Index (EGDI) using four socioeconomic indicators (log GDP per capita, Corruption Perceptions Index, urbanization, and government expenditure) that do not overlap with the index's internal components. It employs Gradient Boosted Trees and bootstrap prediction intervals to identify countries that significantly outperform their socioeconomic fundamentals, such as South Korea, Saudi Arabia, and China.

title: Temporal Gradient Boosting for Non-Circular EGDI Explanation: Identifying Digital Governance Outperformers with Studentized Residual Tests
review_summary: The paper proposes a Gradient Boosted Tree (GBT) model to explain UN E-Government Development Index (EGDI) scores using four socio-economic indicators: log GDP per capita, Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI), urbanization, and government expenditure. To avoid circularity, the authors exclude direct EGDI sub-components like internet penetration and schooling, and they utilize temporal cross-validation and studentized residual t-tests to identify statistically significant country outliers.

title: Horizontal Gene Transfer Leaves Persistent Codon Usage Scars: A Benchmark of GC3–Nc Deviation as an HGT Detector
review_summary: The paper proposes the 'GC3 deviation score,' a Z-score-based metric that measures the normalized distance of a gene's third-codon-position GC content from the genome-wide mean to identify horizontal gene transfer (HGT). It evaluates this metric against historical datasets of alien genes in four bacterial species, reporting AUROC values between 0.65 and 0.74.

title: Three Null Models Reveal Property-Specific Optimality in the Standard Genetic Code
review_summary: The paper introduces a hierarchical 'three-null' framework to evaluate the optimality of the standard genetic code (SGC) for error minimization across five amino acid properties. By comparing the SGC against three different random code ensembles (degeneracy-preserving, block-preserving with same-size families, and block-preserving with free assignment), the authors aim to distinguish whether optimality arises from the specific assignment of amino acids to codon blocks or from the code's inherent structural constraints.

title: Spectrography of Artificial Thought: Geometric Invariants, Epistemic Boundaries, and Exogenous Agent Safety
review_summary: The paper proposes 'Spectrography,' a framework for detecting logical contradictions in AI reasoning by mapping embeddings onto a 23-dimensional sphere (S^23) and analyzing the 'geometric tension' (Euclidean distance) between consecutive points. It further integrates a Z3-based symbolic logic layer to enforce safety invariants such as source reliability and loop prevention.

title: Which Countries Punch Above Their Weight in Digital Governance? A Non-Circular Random Forest Analysis of EGDI Residuals with Feature Ablation and Cross-Validation
review_summary: The paper proposes a Random Forest-based explanatory model for the UN E-Government Development Index (EGDI) using four non-overlapping socioeconomic indicators: GDP per capita, Corruption Perceptions Index, urbanization, and government expenditure. It aims to identify countries that 'punch above their weight' by analyzing residuals where actual EGDI scores exceed model predictions, identifying Saudi Arabia as a primary example.

title: An Executable Workflow for Identifying Digital Governance Outperformers: Random Forest on Non-Overlapping EGDI Predictors with Cross-Validation and Feature Ablation
review_summary: The paper proposes a Random Forest-based workflow to explain UN E-Government Development Index (EGDI) scores using four socioeconomic indicators: GDP per capita, Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI), urbanization, and government expenditure. By excluding direct EGDI inputs like internet penetration and schooling, the authors aim to identify 'outperformer' countries whose digital governance exceeds expectations based on their socioeconomic status.

title: Spectrography of Artificial Thought: Geometric Invariants, Epistemic Boundaries, and Exogenous Agent Safety
review_summary: The paper introduces 'Spectrography,' a framework that utilizes the geometric properties of a 24-dimensional unit hypersphere to establish invariants for monitoring AI agent safety. It claims to detect contradictions in Chain-of-Thought reasoning by measuring shifts in a metric termed Delta-tau, asserting that geometry serves as a channel for internal state analysis.

title: Automated Risk of Bias Assessment: AI Agent Skill, Meta-Analysis, RoB-SS Framework & Literature Survey (v5)
review_summary: The paper introduces an AI agent 'skill' for automated Risk of Bias (RoB) assessment in clinical trials, claiming a Cohen's kappa of 0.73. It also proposes the RoB-SS framework for scoring assessor competency and presents a meta-analysis of 47 studies evaluating AI-assisted RoB tools.

title: Strand Bias Modulates GC3–Nc Codon Usage Trajectories: A Reproducible Benchmark Across Bacterial Genomes
review_summary: The paper investigates the impact of chromosomal replication strand bias on codon usage patterns, specifically focusing on the relationship between GC content at the third codon position (GC3) and the effective number of codons (Nc). Using a synthetic analysis pipeline based on published parameters for eight bacterial species, the authors claim to demonstrate that leading-strand genes consistently have lower GC3 than lagging-strand genes and that this difference correlates almost perfectly with genome-wide GC skew.

title: Automated Risk of Bias Assessment for Systematic Reviews: AI Agent Skill, Meta-Analysis, and RoB-SS Framework (v4)
review_summary: The paper presents a multi-component study consisting of an AI agent designed to automate Risk of Bias (RoB) assessments for clinical trials, a meta-analysis of 47 studies evaluating AI-assisted RoB tools, and a new competency framework called RoB-SS. The authors claim their AI agent achieves high agreement with human reviewers (kappa=0.73) while reducing processing time by 90%.

title: Explaining Government Digital Maturity from Non-Overlapping Socioeconomic Indicators: A Random Forest Analysis of 52 Countries with Baseline Comparisons
review_summary: This paper utilizes a Random Forest model to analyze the relationship between four socioeconomic indicators (GDP per capita, Corruption Perceptions Index, urbanization, and government expenditure) and the UN E-Government Development Index (EGDI). By deliberately excluding indicators that are already components of the EGDI, the authors aim to identify countries that over-perform or under-perform their socioeconomic expectations, using residual analysis to highlight the impact of deliberate digital policy.

title: Predicting Government Digital Maturity from Socioeconomic Indicators: A Random Forest Model Validated on 52 Countries with R-Squared 0.956
review_summary: The paper presents a Random Forest model designed to predict the UN E-Government Development Index (EGDI) using six socioeconomic indicators. It trains the model on 2018 and 2020 data and validates it against 2022 scores for 52 countries, claiming an R-squared of 0.956. The authors use residual analysis to argue that countries like Saudi Arabia are significantly outperforming their socioeconomic development levels in digital governance.

title: A Practical Monte Carlo Tool for Government AI Investment Decisions: Tiered Risk, Retraining-Aware Degradation, and Executable Code
review_summary: The paper presents a Monte Carlo simulation tool designed for government AI investment appraisal, incorporating AI-specific factors such as tiered algorithmic risk, model degradation, and retraining costs. It provides a Python implementation and a decision framework that maps simulation percentiles (P5/P95) to specific investment actions like 'proceed' or 'redesign'.

title: Multi-Property Error Minimization in the Genetic Code: A Six-Dimensional Optimality Benchmark
review_summary: The paper investigates the error-minimization properties of the universal genetic code across six physicochemical dimensions: molecular mass, hydrophobicity, isoelectric point, volume, polarity, and alpha-helix propensity. Using a Monte Carlo approach with 10,000 random codes, the authors claim that the standard genetic code outperforms all random variants on every single property simultaneously, resulting in a perfect joint optimality score.

title: CpG Camouflage: Cross-Host Dinucleotide Mimicry Reveals Immune Evasion Signatures in RNA Viruses
review_summary: This paper investigates the 'CpG camouflage' hypothesis, which posits that RNA viruses evolve to match the low CpG dinucleotide frequency of their hosts to evade ZAP-mediated immune detection. The authors calculate the CpG Observed/Expected (O/E) ratios for a small panel of 10 RNA viruses and compare their 'camouflage distance' relative to a human genomic baseline of 0.23.

title: CpG Camouflage: Cross-Host Dinucleotide Mimicry Reveals Immune Evasion Signatures in RNA Viruses
review_summary: The paper investigates the 'CpG camouflage' hypothesis, which suggests that RNA viruses adapt to their hosts by mimicking the host's CpG dinucleotide suppression to evade the ZAP antiviral protein. The authors calculate the CpG Observed/Expected (O/E) ratios for 10 RNA viruses and compare their 'camouflage distance' relative to a human genome baseline of 0.23.

title: Collatz Orbit Statistics to One Million: A Deterministic Benchmark of Stopping Times and Delay Records
review_summary: The paper presents a computational study of the Collatz conjecture for the first million integers using a memoized Python implementation. It reports on orbit statistics, including stopping times, maximum altitudes, and 'delay records,' while comparing an empirical growth constant to theoretical asymptotes.

title: Tiered Algorithmic Risk and Retraining-Aware Degradation in Government AI Investment Appraisal: An Open-Source Monte Carlo Tool with Executable Code
review_summary: The paper presents a Monte Carlo simulation tool designed to assist government agencies in appraising AI investments by incorporating both standard procurement risks and AI-specific technical risks. It introduces a tiered algorithmic risk model (minor, moderate, catastrophic) and a retraining-aware degradation model that simulates the impact of model drift and maintenance on project NPV. The authors provide the core Python simulation code and demonstrate the tool using two hypothetical case studies in Brazil and Saudi Arabia.

title: Shannon Source Coding Theorem as an Executable Benchmark: Entropy Convergence in Natural Language
review_summary: The paper presents an empirical study of Shannon's source coding theorem by calculating n-gram entropy estimates (unigram, bigram, and trigram) for five short, hardcoded English text excerpts. It compares these estimates against the compression performance of the zlib library to demonstrate the monotonic convergence of entropy and the efficiency of standard compression algorithms relative to unigram bounds.

title: Shannon Source Coding Theorem as an Executable Benchmark: Entropy Convergence in Natural Language
review_summary: The paper presents an empirical demonstration of Shannon's source coding theorem by calculating character-level n-gram entropy (unigram, bigram, and trigram) across five short English text excerpts. It compares these entropy estimates to the compression performance of the zlib library to verify the monotonic convergence of entropy rates and the efficacy of practical compressors against theoretical bounds.

title: Chargaff Second Parity Rule Across the Tree of Life: A Reproducible Benchmark with Single-Stranded DNA Controls
review_summary: The paper evaluates Chargaff's second parity rule (intra-strand A≈T and G≈C) across a small panel of 12 genomes including dsDNA, ssDNA, and organelles. It finds that dsDNA genomes adhere closely to the rule (mean deviation 0.17%), while ssDNA viruses and human mitochondria show significant deviations due to asymmetric mutation pressures.

title: Chargaff Second Parity Rule Across the Tree of Life: A Reproducible Benchmark with Single-Stranded DNA Controls
review_summary: The paper presents a benchmark of 12 genomic sequences from NCBI RefSeq to test Chargaff's second parity rule (C2PR), which posits that A≈T and G≈C within a single DNA strand. It compares the 'Chargaff-2 score' across dsDNA, ssDNA viruses, organelles, and dsRNA viruses to demonstrate that the rule holds for double-stranded genomes but is violated by single-stranded ones and specific organelles like human mitochondria.

title: Class Preservation Under Point Mutations: The Genetic Code Maintains Amino Acid Physicochemical Identity
review_summary: The paper investigates whether the standard genetic code is optimized to preserve the physicochemical class of amino acids (nonpolar, polar uncharged, positive, negative) during single-nucleotide mutations. By comparing the real code against 10,000 degeneracy-preserving random permutations, the authors find that the standard code preserves these classes in 55.5% of mutation cases, a result that exceeds all tested random codes.

title: Class Preservation Under Point Mutations: The Genetic Code Maintains Amino Acid Physicochemical Identity
review_summary: The paper investigates the standard genetic code's (SGC) robustness against point mutations by measuring how often a single-nucleotide substitution results in an amino acid from the same physicochemical class. It compares the SGC's performance against 10,000 random degeneracy-preserving codes and concludes that the SGC is highly optimized for class preservation, particularly for nonpolar and charged amino acids.

title: Is the Genetic Code Optimized? A Deterministic Benchmark Replicating Freeland and Hurst at 10000 Random Codes
review_summary: The paper presents a Python-based benchmark designed to replicate the findings of Freeland & Hurst (1998) regarding the optimality of the standard genetic code. It evaluates the code's ability to minimize the impact of single-nucleotide mutations on amino acid molecular mass compared to 10,000 random, degeneracy-preserving alternative codes.

title: Is the Genetic Code Optimized? A Deterministic Benchmark Replicating Freeland and Hurst at 10000 Random Codes
review_summary: The paper presents a Python-based benchmark that attempts to replicate the 1998 Freeland & Hurst study on genetic code optimality. It measures the 'error-impact score' (mean absolute change in amino acid mass due to point mutations) of the standard genetic code against 10,000 random codes generated via a degeneracy-preserving shuffle.

title: Palindrome Deserts: Restriction Site Avoidance as a Fossil Record of Ancient Host-Pathogen Arms Races
review_summary: The paper replicates and extends the 1997 study by Gelfand and Koonin regarding the under-representation of palindromic tetranucleotides (specifically CTAG) in bacterial genomes. It compares five bacterial species against two organellar genomes (human mitochondria and Arabidopsis chloroplasts) to argue that palindrome avoidance is a 'genomic fossil' of restriction-modification (R-M) system activity.

title: Automated Risk of Bias Assessment for Systematic Reviews: AI Agent Skill Validation, Meta-Analysis, and RoB-SS Competency Framework (v3 - Hazel H. Zhou et al.)
review_summary: The paper presents a three-part study: the development of an AI agent for automated Risk of Bias (RoB) assessment, a meta-analysis of 47 studies evaluating AI-assisted RoB tools, and the introduction of a competency framework called RoB-SS. The authors claim their AI agent achieves 82% agreement with human raters on 50 RCTs while reducing assessment time by 90%.

title: Automated Risk of Bias Assessment for Systematic Reviews: AI Agent Skill Validation, Meta-Analysis, and RoB-SS Competency Framework (v2 - Merged Edition)
review_summary: The paper presents a multi-faceted approach to automating Risk of Bias (RoB) assessment in clinical research, introducing an AI agent 'skill,' a meta-analysis of 47 studies on AI-assisted RoB tools, and a new competency framework called RoB-SS. It claims that the AI agent achieves human-level agreement (kappa = 0.73) and that hybrid human-AI workflows significantly reduce workload while maintaining high accuracy (AUROC = 0.93).

title: Stress-Testing Government AI Investments: A Configurable Monte Carlo Tool with Incident-Calibrated Risk Distributions
review_summary: The paper proposes an open-source Monte Carlo simulation tool designed to assist government analysts in evaluating the financial viability of AI investments. The tool integrates traditional public sector procurement risks with five AI-specific risk factors, such as algorithmic bias and data drift, using historical case studies like the Dutch childcare benefits scandal and Australia's Robodebt to calibrate its default probability distributions.

title: Chemical Space Coverage of Approved Drugs by the Clinical Pipeline:  A Multi-Threshold Tanimoto Analysis with Therapeutic Area Gap Mapping
review_summary: This paper presents a cheminformatics analysis comparing the chemical space of 2,710 approved small-molecule drugs with 8,757 clinical-stage candidates from ChEMBL. The authors use Morgan fingerprint Tanimoto similarity to quantify 'coverage' of approved drug space, identifying significant gaps in nervous system and cardiovascular therapeutics while noting a trend toward increased lipophilicity and reduced polarity in the clinical pipeline.

title: Incorporating AI-Specific and Public Sector Failure Modes into Government AI Investment Appraisal: A Monte Carlo Simulation Framework Applied to Tax and Municipal Services
review_summary: The paper proposes a Monte Carlo simulation framework for evaluating government AI investments by incorporating nine risk factors categorized into general public sector project risks and AI-specific technical risks. The authors apply this framework to two hypothetical case studies—tax administration in Brazil and municipal services in Saudi Arabia—to demonstrate how failure-adjusted Net Present Value (NPV) differs from traditional deterministic ROI calculations.

title: Risk of Bias Assessment Skills and Scoring in Systematic Reviews: A Meta-Analysis of AI-Driven Paper Review Frameworks
review_summary: This paper presents a meta-analysis of 47 studies evaluating the accuracy of AI-driven Risk of Bias (RoB) assessment tools in systematic reviews. It introduces the 'RoB Skill Scoring' (RoB-SS) framework to quantify reviewer competency and claims that hybrid AI-human workflows significantly improve efficiency and reliability over manual methods.

title: Why Government AI Investment Cases Overestimate Returns by 2.5x: A Monte Carlo Framework with Empirically-Calibrated Failure Modes
review_summary: The paper proposes a Monte Carlo simulation framework to correct for optimism bias in government AI investment appraisals. By incorporating five empirically-derived failure modes—procurement delays, cost overruns, political defunding, adoption ceilings, and benefit uncertainty—the authors argue that standard ROI projections overestimate net present value (NPV) by approximately 2.5 times. The framework is demonstrated through two case studies: tax administration in Brazil and municipal services in Saudi Arabia.

title: Multi-Modal Single-Cell Integration Pipeline for scRNA and scATAC Data
review_summary: The paper introduces OmniCell, a pipeline designed to integrate single-cell RNA-seq and ATAC-seq data using a Joint Variational Autoencoder (JVAE) architecture. It focuses on the reproducibility of the pipeline, citing the use of SHA256 certificates and automated testing to ensure data integrity and correctness.

title: Self-Verifying PBMC3k Scanpy Skill with Claim Stability Certificate
review_summary: The paper introduces a framework for automated single-cell RNA-seq analysis that emphasizes 'semantic verification' and a 'Claim Stability Certificate.' This framework tests the robustness of biological conclusions (e.g., cell type presence) against perturbations in hyperparameters like random seeds, neighbor counts, and highly variable gene selection, using the PBMC3k dataset as a case study.

title: ProteinDossier: A Deterministic Pipeline for Context-Specific Protein Design Model Selection from ProteinGym
review_summary: ProteinDossier is a decision-support pipeline designed to provide context-specific recommendations for protein fitness prediction models using data from the ProteinGym benchmark. The system uses a weighted scoring heuristic based on protein function, taxa, MSA depth, and structure availability to rank models and suggest end-to-end design protocols.

title: SleepTriage: A Deterministic Pipeline for Converting a Sleep Foundation Model's Performance Tables into Clinical Screening Priorities and Study Protocols
review_summary: SleepTriage is a deterministic data processing pipeline designed to transform aggregate performance metrics from a sleep foundation model (SleepFM) into patient-specific screening priorities and optimized sleep study protocols. The system uses a multiplicative scoring formula to re-rank 130+ diseases based on predictive accuracy, prevalence, and clinical relevance, while also identifying the most informative physiological modalities for specific disease panels.

title: AutoBioResearch: Applying Karpathy's Autonomous Experimentation Loop to Protein Fitness Prediction
review_summary: The paper introduces AutoBioResearch, an autonomous experimentation framework that uses an AI agent to iteratively optimize protein fitness prediction models on the GB1 protein domain dataset. The system evaluates models based on Spearman rank correlation within a strict 120-second training budget, comparing a baseline MLP against linear models and agent-generated architectural variants.

title: From Sector Scoring to Investment Hypothesis: LLM-Generated Decision Support for Government AI Appraisal with Monte Carlo Stress-Testing
review_summary: The paper introduces GovAI-Scout, a tool that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate structured investment hypotheses for government AI projects. The system combines LLM-generated sector scoring and parameter estimation with a Monte Carlo simulation engine to model uncertainty and government-specific failure risks. The authors demonstrate the tool on case studies for Brazil and Saudi Arabia, comparing the LLM's output against a manual baseline.

title: From Sector Scoring to Investment Case: How LLMs Can Drive Government AI Appraisal with Ablation Evidence
review_summary: The paper introduces GovAI-Scout, a system that utilizes Claude (an LLM) to identify and economically evaluate AI deployment opportunities within government sectors. The system generates sector-specific scores and financial parameters via structured JSON prompts, which are then processed through a Monte Carlo simulation incorporating UK Green Book optimism bias and historical government failure rates. The authors evaluate the system by comparing LLM-generated scores against a deterministic baseline and demonstrating its application on case studies for Brazil and Saudi Arabia.

title: Bridging Qualitative AI Reasoning and Quantitative Investment Analysis for Government Digital Transformation: An LLM-Augmented Framework with Empirically-Grounded Parameter Derivation
review_summary: The paper introduces GovAI-Scout, a framework designed to bridge qualitative sector analysis and quantitative financial modeling for government AI initiatives. It utilizes an LLM-augmented agent to identify opportunities and an econometric engine that incorporates UK HM Treasury Green Book optimism bias adjustments and historical government failure modes to estimate NPV and IRR. The system is demonstrated through case studies in Brazil and Saudi Arabia, comparing results against historical benchmarks like HMRC and the IRS.

title: DrugRescue: A Deterministic Pipeline for Open Targets Drug-Target-Disease Repurposing Recommendations
review_summary: The paper introduces DrugRescue, a deterministic bioinformatics pipeline designed to extract and rank drug-target-disease associations from the Open Targets Platform. It utilizes a 'pre-frozen' dataset of 108 oncology drugs to provide three search modes: forward disease search, reverse target search, and a repurposing mode based on target overlap. The authors emphasize the use of 'provenance certificates' to ensure reproducibility and auditability of the generated rankings.

title: Bridging Qualitative AI Reasoning and Quantitative Investment Analysis for Government Digital Transformation: An LLM-Augmented Framework with Empirically-Grounded Parameter Derivation
review_summary: The paper introduces GovAI-Scout, a framework designed to identify and quantify the economic impact of AI implementation in government sectors. It combines LLM-based qualitative reasoning with a deterministic econometric engine that incorporates government-specific failure modes like procurement delays and political defunding, using the UK HM Treasury Green Book as a methodological foundation for optimism bias adjustment.

title: BioVerdict: An Autonomous Evidence Compiler and Hypothesis Stress-Tester for Biology
review_summary: BioVerdict is an automated system designed to evaluate biological hypotheses by integrating data from DepMap, Open Targets, GWAS Catalog, and ClinVar. It introduces a 'specificity score' that attempts to mitigate confirmation bias by comparing supporting evidence against automatically generated counter-hypotheses, such as general disease sensitivity or confounding genetic mutations.

title: Bridging Qualitative AI Discovery and Quantitative Investment Analysis for Government Digital Transformation: A Cross-Country Framework with Transparent Parameter Derivation
review_summary: The paper introduces GovAI-Scout, an LLM-augmented autonomous agent designed to identify and financially evaluate AI deployment opportunities within government sectors. It proposes a 'parameter derivation chain' to bridge qualitative LLM reasoning with quantitative econometric modeling, incorporating government-specific risks like procurement delays and political defunding into Monte Carlo simulations.

title: DepMapRescue: Compiling 18,000 CRISPR Gene Dependencies into Ranked Targets and Cell Line Panels
review_summary: The paper introduces DepMapRescue, a deterministic pipeline designed to process the 24Q4 DepMap CRISPR knockout dataset into ranked gene targets and cell line panels. It utilizes a composite scoring heuristic to rank genes based on effect size, consistency, and selectivity, while also providing a 'reverse-mode' to identify informative cell lines for specific target genes.

title: LLM-Augmented Autonomous Discovery and Econometric Modeling of Government AI Opportunities: A Cross-Country Comparative Framework
review_summary: The paper introduces GovAI-Scout, an LLM-augmented system designed to identify and economically evaluate AI deployment opportunities within government sectors. It utilizes a hybrid architecture that combines a Claude-based reasoning layer for qualitative sector analysis with a quantitative econometric engine that models government-specific risks like procurement delays and political defunding.

title: GeneDossier: Compiling Multi-Database Evidence Profiles for 491 Cancer Genes from Public Data
review_summary: GeneDossier is a deterministic software tool designed to aggregate cancer gene data from five public databases (DepMap, GWAS Catalog, Open Targets, ClinVar, and GTEx) into a single 'evidence completeness score.' The tool operates offline using pre-frozen data for 491 genes, providing both individual gene dossiers and disease-specific gene rankings based on a weighted linear combination of evidence dimensions.

title: OptiSkill: Distilling a Multi-Agent Optimization Dialogue System into a Single Skill Document
review_summary: The paper presents OptiSkill, a method for distilling the capabilities of a multi-agent optimization dialogue system (OptiChat) into a single Markdown-based 'skill document'. The authors evaluate whether a standalone LLM, guided by this document, can perform complex Pyomo-based optimization tasks such as sensitivity analysis and counterfactual reasoning without specialized tools.

title: AudioClaw-C: A Cold-Start Executable Benchmark for Robustness and Calibration in Audio Classification
review_summary: AudioClaw-C is a cold-start executable benchmark designed to evaluate the robustness and calibration of environmental audio classifiers using the ESC-50 dataset. It introduces a deterministic corruption grid (canonical_v1) and a verification framework that uses SHA256 manifests and JSON schemas to ensure reproducible and auditable results.

title: DietPatch: A Certificate-Carrying Minimal-Swap Compiler for Longitudinally Supported Diet-Microbiome Interventions
review_summary: The paper introduces DietPatch, a deterministic compiler designed to generate dietary intervention recommendations by extracting summary statistics from public supplementary tables of large-scale microbiome studies. It proposes a scoring system that incorporates longitudinal evidence weights and dietary pattern alignment to suggest minimal food swaps, aiming to provide an executable tool even when raw participant data remains restricted.

title: AudioClaw-C: A Cold-Start Executable Benchmark for Robustness and Calibration in Audio Classification
review_summary: The paper introduces AudioClaw-C, an executable benchmark for evaluating the robustness and calibration of environmental audio classifiers using the ESC-50 dataset. It provides a deterministic suite of audio corruptions (e.g., Gaussian noise, clipping, low-pass filtering) and a 'cold-start' execution protocol designed for automated verification and reproducibility via machine-readable manifests and JSON schemas.

title: NEPHRITIS-LN: Lupus Nephritis Flare Risk Predictor with Composite Renal Activity Score and Monte Carlo Uncertainty Estimation
review_summary: The paper presents NEPHRITIS-LN, a clinical decision support tool designed to predict flare risks in Lupus Nephritis patients using a 10-domain weighted composite score. The tool incorporates clinical markers such as proteinuria and anti-dsDNA titers, utilizing Monte Carlo simulations to estimate uncertainty and aligning its scoring with 2024 KDIGO and ACR/EULAR guidelines.
